title: Inherited Religion and What to Do with It
subtitle: A reading on the mid-life process of sorting through what you were given
excerpt: A reading on the process of reviewing the religious framework you were handed in childhood. Covers why the question tends to arrive in mid-life, what the sorting-through process involves, and how people hold onto elements they value while letting go of those that no longer fit.
sourceNotes: Draws on public-domain sociology of religion, particularly research on adult faith transitions (James Fowler's Stages of Faith, 1981), and secularisation studies. Framing is original to homemade.education.

practice.whenToUse: Use when the inherited religious framework is creating friction with adult life and you want context for the process you are in.

content.text: Most people who were raised in a religion have not consciously chosen it. The framework was handed to them before they had the language or the life experience to evaluate it. Mid-life is the most common point at which the review happens, partly because the brain is more comfortable with complexity by that point, and partly because the life events of mid-life (death, crisis, children, illness) raise the questions the childhood framework was built to answer.

Faith development researcher James Fowler described a stage he called Conjunctive Faith, which typically begins in mid-life, characterised by the ability to hold multiple framings at once without needing one to "win". Before that stage, people tend to either accept or reject the inherited framework wholesale. In mid-life, a more nuanced position becomes possible: taking what is useful, questioning what is not, and allowing the not-knowing to coexist with what is kept.
What the sorting process looks like

The sorting process is rarely linear. It tends to involve periods of distance from the inherited framework, then periods of return, then a more settled position that is neither the full childhood faith nor a complete rejection of it. The position most people arrive at in mid-life is a personal synthesis: some elements kept, some let go, some held as questions rather than answers.

The elements most commonly kept are practices (prayer, ritual, community) rather than doctrines. The practices carry meaning that the doctrine debates do not. A person who no longer believes the specific claims of the religion they grew up in may still find the Sunday service, or the candle, or the prayer posture, to be a useful point of return.
What to do when you are in it

The review does not have to be resolved quickly. It can take years. Useful questions to sit with: Which elements of the inherited framework still feel true? Which feel like someone else's answer? Which practices bring something to your life independent of their doctrinal framing?
If the inherited framework came with community, the review may involve separating the beliefs from the people. The community may still belong to your life even when the doctrine no longer does.
Draws on James Fowler's Stages of Faith (Harper & Row, 1981), the sociology of religion literature on adult faith transitions, and original framing for Homemade.
